verses.text: Israel abode in Shittim. The people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab; they called Israel to the sacrifices of their gods. The people did eat, bowed down. Israel joined himself to Baal-peor. The LORD's anger kindled. Guidance: What enemy armies and Balaam's curses could not do, a dinner invitation did. Balaam couldn't curse them, so he taught Moab to seduce them. The path into a foreign altar always passes through a foreign bed. Compromise looks generous before it looks deadly.

Israel joined himself to Baal-peor. The LORD's anger kindled. Guidance: What enemy armies and Balaam's curses could not do, a dinner invitation did. Balaam couldn't curse them, so he taught Moab to seduce them. The path into a foreign altar always passes through a foreign bed. Compromise looks generous before it looks deadly.

The LORD to Moses: take all the heads of the people, hang them up before the LORD against the sun, that the fierce anger may be turned away. Moses to the judges: slay every one his men that were joined unto Baal-peor. Guidance: God commands public accountability — leaders punished in daylight, not behind closed doors. Sin that infected a nation must be confronted at the level it spread. When the heads were exposed, the people understood: the LORD is not amused, and there is no quiet way back.

The LORD to Moses: take all the heads of the people, hang them up before the LORD against the sun, that the fierce anger may be turned away. Moses to the judges: slay every one his men that were joined unto Baal-peor. Guidance: God commands public accountability — leaders punished in daylight, not behind closed doors. Sin that infected a nation must be confronted at the level it spread. When the heads were exposed, the people understood: the LORD is not amused, and there is no quiet way back.

One of Israel brought a Midianite woman in sight of Moses and the congregation, who were weeping at the tabernacle door. Phinehas son of Eleazar rose up, took a javelin, went after them into the tent, thrust both through. The plague was stayed. Those that died: 24,000. Guidance: Public defiance in the middle of national mourning. Phinehas does what the weeping congregation could not bring itself to do — and the plague stops mid-stride. The act was singular, God-named, plague-arresting. It was never a template; it was an emergency.

The LORD: Phinehas hath turned my wrath away from Israel — while he was zealous for my sake, I consumed not Israel in my jealousy. Behold, I give him my covenant of peace — an everlasting priesthood — because he was zealous, and made atonement. Guidance: God names what Phinehas did atonement. One man's holy grief over the camp's sin substituted for the deeper grief God would have rained down. The reward is striking: a covenant of PEACE, given to the man who held the spear. Real zeal is not anger — it is love for God's name fierce enough to act.

The name of the Israelite slain was Zimri son of Salu, a prince of a chief house among the Simeonites. The Midianitish woman was Cozbi, daughter of Zur, head over a people of a chief house in Midian. Guidance: The text names them — not for shame's sake, but to refuse anonymity. A tribal prince and a chieftain's daughter; the rebellion was high-status, not back-row. Sin that hides behind rank is still sin. God writes both names down.

Sin that hides behind rank is still sin. God writes both names down.

The LORD: vex the Midianites, smite them; for they vex you with their wiles, wherewith they have beguiled you in the matter of Peor. Guidance: The seducer is not innocent. Israel sinned by joining; Midian sinned by inviting. God closes the chapter pointing forward to chapter 31, where the Midianite scheme will be answered in kind. Sometimes the kindest thing God does for His people is refuse to let the trap reset behind them.
